name: Zonules.com Claim Registry
note: Every scientific claim is supported by a registered source, marked as conceptual interpretation, marked as internal framework, or excluded. No unsourced scientific claim may be published.

claims.statement: The zonules of Zinn are fine fibrous strands that suspend the crystalline lens from the ciliary body.
The zonular apparatus, also called the suspensory ligament of the lens, holds the lens in centered geometric tension.
Tension transmitted through the zonules allows the ciliary muscle to change lens shape during accommodation, shifting focus across distances.
The structure is named after Johann Gottfried Zinn, the 18th-century anatomist who described it.
The zonules are the literal instance of the suspension layer (FIO-01): focus is produced by hidden structural tension, not by the lens alone.
The ciliary body is a ring of tissue behind the iris that contains the ciliary muscle and gives rise to the zonular fibers.
Contraction of the ciliary muscle reduces tension on the zonules, allowing the elastic lens to become more convex for near focus.
The ciliary body also produces aqueous humor, the fluid that fills the anterior segment of the eye.
The ciliary body is the force source of the suspension layer (FIO-01): it does not focus light but governs the tension that makes focus adjustable.
The crystalline lens is a transparent, biconvex structure suspended behind the iris that refracts light to help form an image on the retina.
The lens is elastic and changes curvature during accommodation.
The lens is enclosed in an elastic capsule into which the zonular fibers insert.
The lens is the focal element of the suspension system: present but unable to focus reliably without zonular tension.
Accommodation is the process by which the eye changes its optical power to maintain a clear image of objects at varying distances.
During accommodation for near vision, the ciliary muscle contracts, zonular tension decreases, and the lens becomes more spherical.
Accommodative ability typically declines with age, a condition known as presbyopia.
Accommodation is the operational expression of FIS-2: focus integrity maintained across an intended operating range.
The lens capsule is a transparent, elastic basement-membrane envelope that surrounds the crystalline lens.
The zonular fibers insert into the lens capsule around the lens equator, transmitting their tension to the lens through the capsule.
The elasticity of the capsule helps mold the lens into a more curved shape when zonular tension is released during accommodation.
The lens capsule is the transmission interface of the suspension layer: the surface through which hidden tension becomes a change in focal geometry.
Visual perception is the process by which the brain organizes and interprets visual input into a stable, meaningful representation of the environment.
Perception is constructive: the visual system actively organizes input rather than passively recording it, as shown by perceptual organization and constancy.
Selective attention determines which parts of the visual field are processed in detail, holding a focal target stable against competing stimuli.

Perception is the Layer 02 instance of the suspension thesis: attention holds a focal interpretation in place the way the zonules hold the lens, and its collapse is the perceptual form of suspension failure (FIO-01).
Machine vision is the field of methods by which artificial systems detect, classify, localize, and interpret content in images and video.
Machine vision systems can produce correct detections while failing to interpret a scene correctly, distinguishing detection from understanding.
Establishing the origin and authenticity of an image is addressed by content provenance standards rather than by detection alone.

Machine vision is the Layer 03 instance of the focus-integrity thesis: calibration and grounding play the suspension role, and machine failures map onto the FIO classes (signal-noise, provenance, interpretation).
